6: vayiqbor oto bagei be'eretz Moav mul Beit-Peor velo-yada ish et-qevuroto ad hayom hazeh (wayyiqbōr ʾōtô baggay bĕʾereṣ mōʾāb mul bêt pĕʿôr wĕlōʾ-yādaʿ ʾîš ʾet-qĕbûrātô ʿad hayyôm hazzeh): 'And he buried him in the valley in the land of Moab opposite Beth-peor; but no one knows the place of his burial to this day.' The subject of the verb vayiqbor (and he buried) is YHWH — Moses is buried by God himself. The unusual hiddenness of Moses's tomb — unknown to his own people — prevented it from becoming a shrine or a site of veneration. Jude 9 mentions that Michael the archangel contended with the devil over Moses's body — suggesting a heavenly concern about where Moses was laid, and why. The contrast with Christ is total: Moses's burial place is unknown and unremembered; Christ's tomb is the most precisely located and historically significant burial site in human history — precisely because it was empty.
10: velo-qam navi od beYisrael keMoshe asher yeda'o YHWH panim el-panim (wĕlōʾ-qām nābîʾ ʿôd bĕyiśrāʾēl kĕmōšeh ʾăšer yĕdāʿô Yhwh pānîm ʾel-pānîm): 'And there has not arisen a prophet since in Israel like Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face.' Deuteronomy closes with a declaration of Moses's prophetic incomparability — and simultaneously with the expectation it creates: the prophet-like-Moses is still to come (Deut 18:15). The closing words of the Torah are simultaneously a tribute to its author-figure and a gap in the narrative: the greatest prophet has died without Israel entering the fullest covenant relationship with YHWH. The NT fulfills this gap: Hebrews 3:3-6 places Jesus above Moses as the Son over the house that Moses served in; John 1:17 contrasts the law given through Moses with grace and truth come through Jesus Christ; and Matthew 17:5 (listen to him — the voice at the Transfiguration) echoes Deut 18:15 (to him you shall listen) and confirms that Jesus is the prophet-like-Moses whom Israel had awaited since the close of Torah.
1: vayyaʿal mōšeh mēʿarḇōt môʾāḇ ʾel-har nĕḇô — 'Moses went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo.' The final ascent: ʿarḇôt môʾāḇ (plains of Moab — the trans-Jordanian plateau where Israel had camped), har nĕḇô (Mount Nebo — the peak of the Abarim range). The peak (rōʾš happisgāh — the summit of Pisgah — H6449, a ridge of the Abarim range) is specified, confirming the view-angle toward the west. The land Moses saw: gilʿād ʿad-dān (Gilead to Dan — the northern Jordan River region), comprising the entire visible extent of the promised land. The Chronicler of Moses's death presents it as a farewell vision — the final gift of seeing what he could not enter.
2: weʾēt kol-naptālî weʾet-ʾerets ʾeprayim ûmanašše — 'And all of Naphtali, the land of Ephraim and Manasseh.' The panoramic list continues: Naphtali's territory in upper Galilee, Ephraim and Manasseh's central hill country, Judah's territory to the western sea (ʾel-hayyām hāʾaḥărôn — to the Mediterranean), the Negev (hannegev), and the Plain of Jericho (kikkar biqʿat yĕrîḥô) to Zoar. The completeness of the vision — from north to south, from east to west — confirms that Moses saw the entire promised inheritance. The vision was not partial but comprehensive: YHWH gave Moses the full inheritance in sight even when denying him the full inheritance in body.
3: weʾet-hannegev weʾet-hakkikkār gê yĕrîḥô — 'The Negev and the Plain, the valley of Jericho, the city of palms, as far as Zoar.' The southern extent: hannegev (the Negev wilderness), kikkār gê yĕrîḥô (the plain/circle of the valley of Jericho — the Jordan valley floor, one of the lowest points on earth), ʿîr hattĕmārîm (city of palms = Jericho, famous for its date palm groves), and ṣōʿar (Zoar — the small city spared in Lot's escape from Sodom, Gen 19:22, at the southern end of the Dead Sea). The complete circuit — from Dan in the north to Zoar in the south — gave Moses a comprehensive survey of the covenant inheritance.
4: vayyōʾmer YHWH ʾēlāyw zōʾt hāʾārets ʾăšer nišbaʿtî lĕʾaḇrāhām lĕyitsḥāq ûlĕyaʿăqōḇ — 'YHWH said to him: This is the land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' The divine speech at the moment of death is YHWH's fulfillment declaration: the land Moses saw was the land of the Abrahamic oath (Gen 12:7; 15:18-21; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13-15). Nišbaʿtî lĕʾaḇrāhām (I swore to Abraham — the Patriarchal covenant) was the legal foundation of Israel's claim to Canaan. The inclusion of all three patriarchal names confirms the covenant's intergenerational continuity: the oath to Abraham was renewed with Isaac and Jacob, creating an unbroken chain of divine commitment. Moses's death did not invalidate the oath — Joshua would carry out what Moses viewed.
5: vayyāmot šāmmāh mōšeh ʿeḇed-YHWH bĕʾerets môʾāḇ — 'Moses died there, the servant of YHWH, in the land of Moab.' The death of Moses is recorded with the title ʿeḇed YHWH (servant of YHWH — H5650, H3068) — the highest possible title in Israel's prophetic tradition. ʿEḇed YHWH would become the name of the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 40-55, the one who would perfectly fulfill what Moses foreshadowed. Moses died ʿal-pî YHWH (by the mouth/command of YHWH — literally 'by the mouth of YHWH'; a rabbinic tradition read this as 'by a divine kiss'). The location — Moab, east of the Jordan — meant Moses died having completed his mission but not entered his reward: the model of the servant who gives everything without personal benefit.
7: ûmōšeh ben-meʾāh weʿeśrîm šānāh bĕmōtô — 'Moses was 120 years old when he died.' The triple structure of Moses's life was recognized in ancient Jewish tradition: 40 years in Egypt, 40 years in Midian, 40 years in the wilderness (Acts 7:23,30,36). The triple perfection (3 × 40) of Moses's 120-year lifespan made his biography a symbol of completeness. Lōʾ-khāhătāh ʿênô welōʾ-nās lēḥōh (his eye was not dim nor had his vigor diminished) — a physiological statement that Moses's death was not from natural decline but from divine appointment. His capacities were fully intact at death — making his inability to enter the land a matter of divine decision, not incapacity.
8: vayyiḇkû ḇnê yisrāʾēl ʾet-mōšeh bĕʿarḇōt môʾāḇ šlōšîm yôm — 'The sons of Israel wept for Moses in the plains of Moab for thirty days.' The mourning period: šelōšîm yôm (thirty days) was the standard royal and prophetic mourning period (cf. Aaron's thirty days in Num 20:29; Saul in 1 Sam 31:13 with seven days). The phrase vayyitmĕû yĕmê vekhi ʾēbel mōšeh (the days of weeping and mourning for Moses were completed) closes the era with formal grief. Moses was mourned as Israel's founder-prophet — the one through whom the nation had been constituted at Sinai. The thirty-day mourning equated Moses with Aaron, honoring both pillars of the wilderness leadership equally in death.
9: wîhôšuaʿ bin-nûn māleʾ rûaḥ ḥokhmāh — 'Joshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom.' The transition: rûaḥ ḥokhmāh (spirit of wisdom — H7307, H2451) was imparted to Joshua when Moses laid hands on him (kî-sāmakh mōšeh ʾet-yādāyw ʿālāyw — because Moses had laid his hands on him). The spirit of wisdom for governance was distinct from the prophetic spirit — it equipped Joshua for the specific task of conquest administration and tribal distribution. Vayyišmĕʿû ʾēlāyw bĕnê yisrāʾēl (the sons of Israel obeyed him) — the leadership transfer was complete and accepted. YHWH's command to obey Joshua (Josh 1:16-18) was already anticipated in Israel's voluntary submission.
11: lĕkhol-hāʾōtôt wĕhammôpĕṯîm ʾăšer šĕlaḥô YHWH laʿăśôt bĕʾerets mitsrāyim — 'For all the signs and wonders YHWH sent him to do in Egypt.' The summary of Moses's unique qualification: the ʾōtôt (signs — H226) and môpĕṯîm (wonders — H4159) in Egypt against Pharaoh (lĕpharʿōh ûlĕkhol-ʿăḇādāyw ûlĕkhol-ʾartsô). The ten plagues, the parting of the Reed Sea, the provision of manna and water — these constituted Moses's prophetic credential. No prophet before or after equaled these signs in scope or directness (lekhol yad hăḥăzāqāh ûlĕkhol hammôrāʾ hāggādōl — all the mighty hand and great terror, v34:12). The conclusion establishes Moses as the unequaled prophet of the Old Covenant, awaiting his antitype.
12: ûlĕkhol hayyād hahăzāqāh ûlĕkhol hammôrāʾ hāggādôl — 'And for all the mighty hand and all the great terror that Moses did in the sight of all Israel.' The closing words of the Pentateuch: the mighty hand (yaḏ hahăzāqāh — H3027, H2389) is the formula used for the Exodus deliverance throughout Deuteronomy (4:34; 5:15; 6:21; 7:8; 11:2; 26:8). Moses performed these wonders lĕʿênê kol-yisrāʾēl (in the sight of all Israel) — a public, witnessed, verifiable revelation. The Pentateuch ends where it must: at Moses's death, with the covenant in place, the land in sight, and the prophetic office awaiting its fulfillment. The Mosaic era closes expecting its successor — both the immediate Joshua and the ultimate One of whom Moses said 'YHWH your God will raise up a prophet like me' (Deut 18:15).
